summary: O'Neil argues that data journalism is becoming essential as data proliferates into all corners of culture, making statistical literacy a requirement for journalists. She emphasizes that coding skills alone aren't sufficient—journalists must also be skeptical of models and data. The post highlights how opaque, proprietary algorithms provide perfect cover for discriminatory intentions that would be obvious if done outright. O'Neil proposes that newsrooms form teams with combined computing and investigative expertise, pointing to ProPublica's reverse-engineering of Obama's messaging model as an exemplar of what good data journalism looks like.
key_points: Basic data and computing literacy is becoming vital for journalism as data proliferates into all aspects of culture
Learning to code is necessary but not sufficient—skepticism of models and data is equally important
Opaque, proprietary models provide 'a perfect hiding place for bad intentions' where discrimination can be embedded and never discovered
Newsrooms need teams that collectively have computing and investigative expertise, not impossible do-everything individuals
Without data-literate journalism, problematic models will go unchallenged
money_quotes: The proliferation of data is leaking into all corners of our culture, and basic data and computing literacy is becoming increasingly vital to the job of journalism.
If you don't learn to code, and even more importantly if you don't learn to be skeptical of the models and the data, then you will have yet another obstacle between you and the truth.
In this day and age, we have complicated, opaque, and proprietary models: in other words, a perfect hiding place for bad intentions.

It would be idiotic for someone with the intention of being discriminatory to do so outright. It's much easier to embed such a thing in an opaque model where it will seem unintentional and will probably never be discovered at all.
The alternative is that the models go unchallenged, which is a really bad idea.
key_insight: Opaque algorithms have become the perfect hiding place for discriminatory intent, and only data-literate investigative journalism can challenge them.
